meta_title: zkCoins — Private Bitcoin via Shielded CSV
meta_description: zkCoins brings true privacy to Bitcoin via Shielded CSV: amounts, sender, receiver and history stay off-chain, proven with zero-knowledge. Each transfer publishes only a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin — about 16 vBytes on-chain. No soft fork, self-custodial. Built on the paper by Jonas Nick, Liam Eagen and Robin Linus (ePrint 2025/068).
og_title: zkCoins — Private Bitcoin via Shielded CSV
og_description: True privacy for Bitcoin via Shielded CSV — coin data stays off-chain, with each transfer publishing only a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin. No soft fork, self-custodial.
hero_lede: zkCoins brings true privacy to Bitcoin. Amounts, sender, receiver and history stay off-chain — proven with zero-knowledge, while each transfer publishes only a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin.
tx_public_foot: ~140 vBytes · fully public · one per payment
compare_cap: Coin data never touches the chain. Each transfer publishes a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin — about 16 vBytes, versus ~140 for a regular transfer — constant no matter how many coins it moves.
quote_text: "Use the chain for what the chain is good for — an immutable ordering of commitments to prevent double-spending."
quote_cite: — Robin Linus, co-author of Shielded CSV
paper_title: Shielded CSV: Private and Efficient Client-Side Validation
paper_subtitle: A privacy-preserving variant of Client-Side Validation, settled directly on Bitcoin.
meta_authors_dd: Jonas Nick (Blockstream) · Liam Eagen (Alpen Labs) · Robin Linus (ZeroSync)
meta_predecessor_dd: zkCoins (Robin Linus, 2023) · builds on the ZeroSync prototype
paper_abstract: Client-Side Validation decouples transaction validation from Bitcoin consensus by moving it to the recipient. Shielded CSV improves on prior CSV designs by hiding the transaction history entirely: each transfer leaks only a 64-byte nullifier to the chain, with the rest verified client-side via succinct zero-knowledge proofs. The protocol is compatible with Bitcoin as-is — no soft fork, no hard fork — and targets roughly an order of magnitude more transactions per second than the base layer alone.
paper_note: zkCoins v1 implements the paper's model faithfully: each transfer publishes its ~64-byte nullifier directly to Bitcoin as a half-aggregated signature, so the chain itself guarantees every nullifier a verifier needs for its double-spend checks — no off-chain batch data, no data-availability assumption. About 16 vBytes on-chain per transfer, on the order of ~100 transactions per second.
changes_h2: Private transfers, a tiny nullifier on Bitcoin
changes_p: Coin data stays off-chain. Each transfer publishes only a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin, proven with zero-knowledge — so privacy and a small on-chain footprint win at once.
concept1_v: Coin data never touches the chain. Each transfer publishes a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin — nothing legible about amounts or parties.
concept2_v: Unlike RGB or Taproot Assets, history stays hidden under a zero-knowledge proof. Recipients verify validity without ever seeing the lineage.
concept3_v: No soft fork, no hard fork. CSV protocols use the chain only for ordering and double-spend prevention.
concept4_h: A fraction of a Bitcoin transfer
concept4_v: Each transfer costs about 16 vBytes on-chain — a fraction of a ~140-vByte Bitcoin transfer, roughly an order of magnitude smaller — constant no matter how many coins it moves.
how_h2: The chain orders, the recipient validates
how_p: Traditional blockchains ask every node to validate every transaction. Shielded CSV inverts that — validation moves to the recipient, and Bitcoin does only what it is uniquely good at.
step1_d: The sender creates a transfer and generates a succinct validity proof of the coin's entire history — a constant size, no matter how old the coin is.
step2_d: The proof travels directly to the receiver, off-chain. Only sender and receiver ever see the amounts, parties and history — no amounts or parties are written to the chain.
step3_t: The nullifier is published to Bitcoin
step3_d: The transfer's ~64-byte nullifier is published to Bitcoin as a half-aggregated signature. Publishing is permissionless — any wallet can self-publish — and the chain itself holds every nullifier a verifier needs.
step4_d: The receiver re-verifies the coin's full zero-knowledge proof locally — a single constant-time check, no matter how long the coin's history — and accepts it without trusting any third party.
step5_d: Bitcoin records each nullifier in canonical order, and the first on-chain occurrence of a coin's state key selects its one valid transition. The chain's one job: guarantee each coin is spent exactly once — with no off-chain data required.
boundaries_p: Privacy here is structural, not a bolt-on. It is worth being precise about what this protocol deliberately is not.
not1_p: It uses Bitcoin L1 directly — no separate consensus, no bridge to a foreign chain.
not2_p: No sequencer, no data-availability layer — the nullifiers live on Bitcoin itself. Validity proofs move peer-to-peer between sender and receiver; each transfer's ~64-byte nullifier anchors to Bitcoin L1.
not3_p: Privacy is structural, not obfuscation — a global anonymity set of every coin, not a small per-round pool.
not4_p: No native protocol token to bootstrap. Value lives in client-side-validated coins.
not5_p: It works on Bitcoin as it exists today — no consensus change, no new opcodes.
phase01_lede: The proving stack and the wallet–server boundary. Everything downstream depends on this.
phase01_i1_why: Move the state-transition circuit onto a recursion-native proving system with a SNARK-friendly hash.
phase01_i2_why: Single-host Apple Silicon target — no GPU farms, no cloud proving service. The wallet holds only the private key.
phase01_i3_why: The wallet signs a SHA-256 digest of the server-computed state. No in-browser zero-knowledge.
phase01_i4_why: Create account, mint, send, receive — the full user loop runs on a public Bitcoin testnet.
phase02_lede: Close the protocol gaps between "works on signet" and "safe with real Bitcoin".
phase02_i1_why: Eliminate the residual link between sender, recipient and coin identifier.
phase02_i2_why: Gracefully degrade when the canonical chain reorganises rather than getting stuck.
phase02_i3_why: Recipients verify a coin's age locally without trusting any third party.
phase02_i4_why: The full set of formal properties from the Shielded CSV paper, encoded as failing-witness tests.
phase03_lede: Mint and burn against native BTC without trusting the issuer. The biggest user-facing trust gap closes here.
phase03_i1_why: 1-of-N honesty trust model. The same construction running on Citrea mainnet.
phase03_i2_why: A succinct proof that a deposit landed at depth ≥ 6 in the canonical chain.
phase03_i3_why: Bridge our recursion-friendly proofs into a verifier that Bitcoin script can check.
phase03_i4_why: Start with a single-operator federation; grow to one hundred independent members, where one honest key deletion is enough.
phase03_i5_why: Full peg-in / peg-out roundtrip including a deliberately malicious operator scenario.
phase04_lede: Move value between Lightning and zkCoins atomically — no provider trust, no custodial bridge.
phase04_i1_what: HTLC on the nullifier funding transaction.
phase04_i1_why: Atomicity lives on Bitcoin layer 1, not on the private coin layer. Standard primitives, no soft fork.
phase04_i2_why: Buy with Lightning sats and sell back to Lightning sats, both directions trustless.
phase04_i3_why: Anyone can run a swap provider; the protocol enforces honesty, not the operator.
phase04_i4_why: A swap provider learns the LN side, never the zkCoins-internal graph.
phase05_lede: Replace the bridge technologies of today with the bridge technologies of tomorrow — without changing the protocol.
phase05_i1_why: Port to a smaller field with a faster hash once the upstream stack stabilises. Algorithmic structure stays.
phase05_i2_why: Drop-in successors to BitVM2 (Glock, Mosaic) cut on-chain dispute cost by two orders of magnitude. Circuit contract unchanged.
phase05_i3_why: Recruit independent organisations as federation members until one-honest-deletion is a meaningful assumption.
phase05_i4_why: A 30–60 contributor ceremony replaces the single-contributor Groth16 parameter set used to test the bridge verifier — zkCoins' own proofs are transparent (FRI) and need no setup.
roadmap_note: Note. Dates indicate target windows for the engineering work, not a guaranteed mainnet date. zkCoins runs in a public test environment until pre-mainnet hardening completes; no real Bitcoin is at risk before then.
investors_title: Direct channel for funds and angels
investors_intro: zkCoins brings Shielded CSV to a self-custodial wallet — private Bitcoin on L1, no soft fork. We keep an open line for venture funds, family offices and strategic angels evaluating the project.
investors_body: The protocol is built on published research (ePrint 2025/068), the stack is open source, and the roadmap is public. We are not actively raising — but we are open to conversations ahead of mainnet.
faq_a1: zkCoins is a self-custodial wallet for Shielded CSV, a privacy protocol for Bitcoin. Amounts, sender, receiver and transaction history stay off-chain, proven with zero-knowledge and verified client-side. Each transfer publishes only a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin.
faq_q2: Is zkCoins a new token or altcoin?
faq_a2: No — zkCoins is not a separate blockchain, altcoin, or native protocol token. It is a client-side-validation layer anchored to Bitcoin L1; value lives in client-side-validated coins. Moving on-chain BTC in and out uses a bridge, which is on the roadmap.
faq_q3: Does zkCoins require a soft fork?
faq_a3: No. Shielded CSV works on Bitcoin as it exists today — no soft fork, no hard fork, no new opcodes. The chain is used only for ordering commitments and preventing double-spends.
faq_q4: How does zkCoins keep transactions private?
faq_a4: Each spend is proven with a zero-knowledge proof and shared off-chain. Coin data — amounts, parties and history — never touches Bitcoin; only a ~64-byte nullifier per transfer is published to the chain.
faq_a5: Yes. Keys are generated and stored locally in your browser and never leave your device. zkCoins is peer-to-peer with no coordinator or custodian that can freeze funds or shut the network down.
faq_a6: Shielded CSV (Client-Side Validation) is a privacy-preserving Bitcoin protocol from the paper by Jonas Nick, Liam Eagen and Robin Linus (ePrint 2025/068). zkCoins v1 implements it faithfully: each transfer publishes a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin, everything else verified client-side.
faq_q7: Is the publisher role actually decentralized?
faq_a7: Publishing is permissionless, and any wallet can self-publish its own transfers to Bitcoin at trivial cost — that caps what a dominant publisher can charge. Competitive economics do favor the fastest, best-capitalised operator, so some concentration is expected. It's a liveness risk only: a publisher never holds custody and can at most censor or delay a transfer, never steal or forge coins. Full breakdown on the docs' Risks page.
faq_a7_json: Publishing is permissionless, and any wallet can self-publish its own transfers to Bitcoin at trivial cost — that caps what a dominant publisher can charge. Competitive economics do favor the fastest, best-capitalised operator, so some concentration is expected. It's a liveness risk only: a publisher never holds custody and can at most censor or delay a transfer, never steal or forge coins. Full breakdown on the docs' Risks page.
faq_a8: On Bitcoin. Each transfer publishes its ~64-byte nullifier directly to the chain as a half-aggregated signature, so Bitcoin itself guarantees the availability of every nullifier a verifier needs to check for double-spends — no off-chain batch data and no separate data-availability layer to trust. The coin data — amounts, parties and history — still stays off-chain, proven with zero-knowledge, and is never needed to reconstruct the public double-spend view.
cta_p: The zkCoins wallet runs in your browser. Keys are generated and stored locally — they never leave your device. Currently on a public test network; no real Bitcoin is at risk.
footer_tag: Private Bitcoin transactions via Shielded CSV. Settled on Bitcoin, verified client-side.
jsonld_org_description: zkCoins is a self-custodial wallet for Shielded CSV — private, client-side-validated Bitcoin transactions anchored on Bitcoin L1.
jsonld_website_description: Private Bitcoin transactions via Shielded CSV. Coin data stays off-chain — each transfer publishes only a ~64-byte nullifier to Bitcoin.
jsonld_wallet_description: Self-custodial wallet for sending and receiving private Bitcoin via the Shielded CSV protocol. Keys are generated and stored locally in the browser.
